With the parameters estimated, we can write the fitted model as a concrete formula:

$$\widehat{P(\text{Voted} = 1)} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\eta}}$$

where the linear predictor $\eta$ is

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta = \, & -2.43 + 0.012 \cdot \text{Age} + 0.036 \cdot \text{sexMale} \\
&{} + 0.086 \cdot \text{Civic Duty} + 0.071 \cdot \text{Hawthorne} \\
&{} + 0.202 \cdot \text{Self} + 0.359 \cdot \text{Neighbors} \\
&{} + 0.820 \cdot \text{Sometimes Vote} + 1.610 \cdot \text{Always Vote} \\
&{} + 0.031 \cdot (\text{Civic Duty} \times \text{Sometimes}) + 0.059 \cdot (\text{Hawthorne} \times \text{Sometimes}) \\
&{} + 0.053 \cdot (\text{Self} \times \text{Sometimes}) + 0.041 \cdot (\text{Neighbors} \times \text{Sometimes}) \\
&{} - 0.051 \cdot (\text{Civic Duty} \times \text{Always}) + 0.063 \cdot (\text{Hawthorne} \times \text{Always}) \\
&{} - 0.006 \cdot (\text{Self} \times \text{Always}) + 0.006 \cdot (\text{Neighbors} \times \text{Always})
\end{aligned}$$

Each `treatment*` term is a 0/1 dummy: 1 if the voter received that postcard, 0 otherwise. Each `voter_class*` term is also a dummy. The interaction terms are products of two dummies --- 1 only if the voter has both that treatment and that voter class. The intercept (-2.43) is the log-odds for a 0-year-old female Rarely Vote voter assigned No Postcard --- mathematically defined but not a sensible prediction; the intercept is a reference point, not a forecast.

**This is our data generating mechanism.**

###

A data generating mechanism is just a formula, something we can write down and implement with computer code. The non-linearity ($1/(1 + e^{-\eta})$) and the seventeen parameters together are why coefficient-by-coefficient interpretation is awkward; `marginaleffects` in Temperance gives us the cleaner reading on the outcome scale.

### Exercise 14

Create a new code chunk in your QMD with `#| cache: true`. Copy/paste the code for estimating the model:

```
fit_shaming <- logistic_reg(engine = "glm") |>
  fit(voted ~ age + sex + treatment*voter_class, data = x)
```

`Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + K`. At the R prompt, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r courage-14}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "    > show_file(\"analysis.qmd\", chunk = \"Last\")\n    ```{r}\n    #| cache: true\n    fit_shaming <- logistic_reg(engine = \"glm\") |>\n      fit(voted ~ age + sex + treatment*voter_class, data = x)\n    ```",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 8)
```

###

`#| cache: true` tells Quarto to reuse the saved fit on subsequent renders. Re-render with `Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + K` --- it should be quick the second time. Even at 344K rows our fit takes only about a second, but caching is the right habit to build for the heavier models you'll meet later in the curriculum.

### Exercise 15

Add `*_cache` to your `.gitignore`. Cached objects don't belong on GitHub.

At the R prompt, run:

```
show_file(".gitignore")
```

CP/CR.

```{r courage-15}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "    > show_file(\".gitignore\")\n    analysis_files\n    *_cache",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 5)
```

###

Commit and push.

### Exercise 16

At the R prompt, run `tidy()` on `fit_shaming` with `conf.int = TRUE`.

```{r courage-16, exercise = TRUE}

```

```{r courage-16-hint-1, eval = FALSE}
tidy(fit_shaming, conf.int = TRUE)
```

```{r courage-16-test, include = FALSE}
tidy(fit_shaming, conf.int = TRUE)
```

###

`tidy()` is part of **[broom](https://broom.tidymodels.org/)**, used to summarize information from a wide variety of models. The `estimate`, `conf.low`, and `conf.high` columns are all on the log-odds scale --- the same scale as the model's link function.

### Exercise 17

Create a new code chunk in your QMD. Ask AI to make a nice-looking table from the tibble returned by `tidy()`. Insert your table code, `Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + K`, and at the R prompt run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r courage-17}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "    > show_file(\"analysis.qmd\", chunk = \"Last\")\n    ```{r}\n    tidy(fit_shaming, conf.int = TRUE) |>\n      select(term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high) |>\n      knitr::kable(\n        caption = \"Logistic-regression estimates (log-odds scale) and 95% CIs.  Source: Gerber, Green, Larimer 2008 via primer.data::shaming\",\n        digits = 3\n      )\n    ```",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 10)
```

###

A nice table includes a title, a caption with the data source, and reasonable rounding. For a 17-parameter logistic regression, three decimal places keeps the small interaction coefficients visible without over-precising the larger main-effect ones.

### Exercise 18

Add a sentence to your project summary explaining the model structure.

```{r courage-18}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "We model voter turnout as a Bernoulli variable which is a logistic function of treatment, voter class, the treatment-by-voter-class interaction, age, and sex.",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 5)
```

###

Add your sentence to the summary paragraph. `Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + K`, then commit/push.


## Temperance
###

Courage provides the data generating mechanism. We can express the DGM in four ways:

**In words:** We model voter turnout as a Bernoulli variable which is a logistic function of treatment, voter class, the treatment-by-voter-class interaction, age, and sex.

**In R code:**

```r
fit_shaming <- logistic_reg(engine = "glm") |>
  fit(voted ~ age + sex + treatment*voter_class, data = x)
```

**In a parameter table:**

```{r}
#| echo: false
fit_shaming |>
  tidy(conf.int = TRUE) |>
  select(term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(v) round(v, 3)))
```

**As a mathematical formula:**

$$\widehat{P(\text{Voted} = 1)} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\eta}}$$

where the linear predictor $\eta$ is

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta = \, & -2.43 + 0.012 \cdot \text{Age} + 0.036 \cdot \text{sexMale} \\
&{} + 0.086 \cdot \text{Civic Duty} + 0.071 \cdot \text{Hawthorne} \\
&{} + 0.202 \cdot \text{Self} + 0.359 \cdot \text{Neighbors} \\
&{} + 0.820 \cdot \text{Sometimes Vote} + 1.610 \cdot \text{Always Vote} \\
&{} + 0.031 \cdot (\text{Civic Duty} \times \text{Sometimes}) + 0.059 \cdot (\text{Hawthorne} \times \text{Sometimes}) \\
&{} + 0.053 \cdot (\text{Self} \times \text{Sometimes}) + 0.041 \cdot (\text{Neighbors} \times \text{Sometimes}) \\
&{} - 0.051 \cdot (\text{Civic Duty} \times \text{Always}) + 0.063 \cdot (\text{Hawthorne} \times \text{Always}) \\
&{} - 0.006 \cdot (\text{Self} \times \text{Always}) + 0.006 \cdot (\text{Neighbors} \times \text{Always})
\end{aligned}$$

### Exercise 1

In your own words, describe the use of Temperance in data science.

```{r temperance-1}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "Temperance interprets the data generating mechanism and then uses it to answer, with the help of graphics, the question(s) with which we began. Humility reminds us that this answer is always false.",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 5)
```

###

Courage gave us the DGM, including its 17 estimated parameters. Temperance puts those parameters to work --- not by interpreting them coefficient-by-coefficient (a hopeless task on the log-odds scale with eight interaction terms), but by using **[marginaleffects](https://marginaleffects.com/)** to translate the model into outcome-scale answers. The companion book is *[Model to Meaning](https://marginaleffects.com/)* by Vincent Arel-Bundock.

### Exercise 2

Add `library(marginaleffects)` to the QMD file. Place your cursor on that line. Use `Cmd/Ctrl + Enter`.

CP/CR.

```{r temperance-2}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "    > library(marginaleffects)\n    >",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 3)
```

###

In the end, we don't really care about parameters, much less how to interpret seventeen of them. Parameters are *imaginary*, like unicorns. We care about answers to our questions, on the outcome scale.

### Exercise 3

What is the specific question we are trying to answer?

```{r temperance-3}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "What is the average causal effect of the Neighbors postcard, relative to no postcard, on the probability that a registered voter casts a ballot in the August 2006 Michigan primary?",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 4)
```

###

A specific question must specify a number to compute. *"How does the postcard treatment affect turnout?"* is too vague. *"The average causal effect of Neighbors versus No Postcard on the probability of voting"* is one specific number we can produce.

### Exercise 4

`avg_comparisons()` computes average causal effects on the outcome scale. At the R prompt, run:

```
avg_comparisons(extract_fit_engine(fit_shaming), variables = "treatment")
```

The `extract_fit_engine()` step is required because `marginaleffects` needs the underlying `glm` object, not the `parsnip` wrapper.

CP/CR.

```{r temperance-4}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "    > avg_comparisons(extract_fit_engine(fit_shaming), variables = \"treatment\")\n\n                     Contrast Estimate Std. Error     z Pr(>|z|)     S  2.5 % 97.5 %\n     Civic Duty - No Postcard   0.0183    0.00253  7.24   <0.001  41.0 0.0133 0.0232\n     Hawthorne - No Postcard    0.0254    0.00253 10.04   <0.001  76.4 0.0204 0.0303\n     Neighbors - No Postcard    0.0811    0.00261 31.08   <0.001 702.2 0.0760 0.0862\n     Self - No Postcard         0.0482    0.00257 18.76   <0.001 258.5 0.0432 0.0533\n\n    Term: treatment\n    Type: response",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 12)
```

```{r temperance-4-test, include = FALSE}
avg_comparisons(extract_fit_engine(fit_shaming), variables = "treatment")
```

###

Each row is the average difference in *predicted probability of voting* between that treatment arm and No Postcard, on the response scale (not the log-odds scale of the parameter table). Reading the four contrasts: Neighbors raises turnout by about 8.1 percentage points; Self by about 4.8; Hawthorne by about 2.5; Civic Duty by about 1.8. *Percentage points*, not *percent*: a difference of 8.1 percentage points (from 30% to 38.1% baseline turnout) is about a 27% relative increase, but those are different numbers.

### Exercise 5

Now break the comparisons down by voter class. At the R prompt, run:

```
avg_comparisons(extract_fit_engine(fit_shaming),
                variables = "treatment",
                by = "voter_class")
```

CP/CR.

```{r temperance-5}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "    > avg_comparisons(extract_fit_engine(fit_shaming),\n    +                 variables = \"treatment\",\n    +                 by = \"voter_class\")\n\n                     Contrast    voter_class Estimate Std. Error\n     Civic Duty - No Postcard Rarely Vote     0.00948    0.00500\n     Hawthorne - No Postcard  Rarely Vote     0.00774    0.00504\n     Neighbors - No Postcard  Rarely Vote     0.04369    0.00549\n     Self - No Postcard       Rarely Vote     0.02327    0.00519\n     Civic Duty - No Postcard Sometimes Vote  0.02382    0.00323\n     Hawthorne - No Postcard  Sometimes Vote  0.02649    0.00322\n     Neighbors - No Postcard  Sometimes Vote  0.08590    0.00334\n     Self - No Postcard       Sometimes Vote  0.05355    0.00330\n     Civic Duty - No Postcard Always Vote     0.00859    0.00577\n     Hawthorne - No Postcard  Always Vote     0.03301    0.00578\n     Neighbors - No Postcard  Always Vote     0.09041    0.00579\n     Self - No Postcard       Always Vote     0.04872    0.00576\n\n    Term: treatment\n    Type: response",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 18)
```

```{r temperance-5-test, include = FALSE}
avg_comparisons(extract_fit_engine(fit_shaming),
                variables = "treatment",
                by = "voter_class")
```

###

This is the heterogeneous-treatment-effect picture. The Neighbors postcard moves *Rarely Vote* voters by only about 4.4 percentage points, but moves *Sometimes Vote* and *Always Vote* voters by 8.6 and 9.0 percentage points respectively. Counterintuitively, the postcard has a *smaller* effect on the people who needed a nudge most (Rarely Vote) and a *larger* effect on people who were already inclined to vote anyway. The campaign-allocation implication is meaningful: targeting the Sometimes/Always voters with Neighbors is the most cost-effective use of a fixed mailing budget.

### Exercise 6

`plot_predictions(condition = c(...))` draws the model's predicted probabilities across two covariates simultaneously. At the R prompt, run:

```
plot_predictions(extract_fit_engine(fit_shaming),
                 condition = c("treatment", "voter_class"))
```

CP/CR.

```{r temperance-6}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "    > plot_predictions(extract_fit_engine(fit_shaming),\n    +                  condition = c(\"treatment\", \"voter_class\"))\n\n(A plot opens with three panels --- one per voter class --- each showing predicted turnout for the five treatment arms with confidence intervals. Always Vote sits highest (around 60-70%); Sometimes Vote in the middle (about 30-40%); Rarely Vote lowest (about 10-15%). Within each panel, the Neighbors postcard sits clearly above the others.)",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 7)
```

```{r temperance-6-test, include = FALSE}
plot_predictions(extract_fit_engine(fit_shaming),
                 condition = c("treatment", "voter_class"))
```

###

Two-variable conditioning shows the effect surface of the model: predicted probability across both axes at once. The size of the gap between Neighbors and No Postcard varies visibly across the three voter classes, which is exactly what the interaction term in the model captures.

### Exercise 7

Run the same call with `draw = FALSE` to see the underlying tibble.

CP/CR.

```{r temperance-7}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "    > plot_predictions(extract_fit_engine(fit_shaming),\n    +                  condition = c(\"treatment\", \"voter_class\"),\n    +                  draw = FALSE)\n\n  rowid  estimate ...   treatment    voter_class  conf.low  conf.high\n1     1     0.123 ...  No Postcard    Rarely Vote     0.118      0.128\n2     2     0.132 ...   Civic Duty    Rarely Vote     0.122      0.143\n...                                                              \n15   15     0.687 ...    Neighbors    Always Vote     0.682      0.692",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 7)
```

```{r temperance-7-test, include = FALSE}
plot_predictions(extract_fit_engine(fit_shaming),
                 condition = c("treatment", "voter_class"),
                 draw = FALSE)
```

###

`plot_predictions()` returns a ggplot object, but it's often easier to work from the underlying tibble (`draw = FALSE`) and build the final plot from scratch.

### Exercise 8

Work with AI to create a beautiful plot starting from the output of `plot_predictions(..., draw = FALSE)`. Do this in your QMD. Title: key variables. Subtitle: takeaway. Caption: data source. Y-axis: percentages. Paste the plot code below.

```{r temperance-8}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "    plot_predictions(extract_fit_engine(fit_shaming),\n                     condition = c(\"treatment\", \"voter_class\"),\n                     draw = FALSE) |>\n      ggplot(aes(x = treatment, y = estimate, color = voter_class)) +\n      geom_pointrange(aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high),\n                      size = 0.7) +\n      scale_x_discrete(limits = c(\"No Postcard\", \"Civic Duty\", \"Hawthorne\",\n                                  \"Self\", \"Neighbors\")) +\n      scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_percent(),\n                         limits = c(0, 0.75)) +\n      facet_wrap(~ voter_class) +\n      labs(\n        title    = \"Predicted Turnout by Postcard and Voter Class\",\n        subtitle = \"Neighbors moves Sometimes/Always voters most; Rarely Vote barely budges\",\n        x        = NULL,\n        y        = NULL,\n        color    = \"Voter Class\",\n        caption  = \"Source: Gerber, Green, Larimer 2008 via primer.data::shaming\"\n      ) +\n      theme_minimal() +\n      theme(legend.position = \"none\",\n            axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 22)
```

###

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
plot_predictions(extract_fit_engine(fit_shaming),
                 condition = c("treatment", "voter_class"),
                 draw = FALSE) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = treatment, y = estimate, color = voter_class)) +
  geom_pointrange(aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high),
                  size = 0.7) +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("No Postcard", "Civic Duty", "Hawthorne",
                              "Self", "Neighbors")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_percent(),
                     limits = c(0, 0.75)) +
  facet_wrap(~ voter_class) +
  labs(
    title    = "Predicted Turnout by Postcard and Voter Class",
    subtitle = "Neighbors moves Sometimes/Always voters most; Rarely Vote barely budges",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = NULL,
    color    = "Voter Class",
    caption  = "Source: Gerber, Green, Larimer 2008 via primer.data::shaming"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))
```

### Exercise 9

Finalize the new graphics chunk in your QMD. `Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + K`. At the R prompt, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r temperance-9}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "    > show_file(\"analysis.qmd\", chunk = \"Last\")\n    ```{r}\n    plot_predictions(extract_fit_engine(fit_shaming),\n                     condition = c(\"treatment\", \"voter_class\"),\n                     draw = FALSE) |>\n      ggplot(aes(x = treatment, y = estimate, color = voter_class)) +\n      geom_pointrange(aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), size = 0.7) +\n      scale_x_discrete(limits = c(\"No Postcard\", \"Civic Duty\", \"Hawthorne\",\n                                  \"Self\", \"Neighbors\")) +\n      scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_percent(), limits = c(0, 0.75)) +\n      facet_wrap(~ voter_class) +\n      labs(title = \"Predicted Turnout by Postcard and Voter Class\",\n           subtitle = \"Neighbors moves Sometimes/Always voters most; Rarely Vote barely budges\",\n           x = NULL, y = NULL, color = \"Voter Class\",\n           caption = \"Source: Gerber, Green, Larimer 2008 via primer.data::shaming\") +\n      theme_minimal() +\n      theme(legend.position = \"none\", axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))\n    ```",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 18)
```

###

The map is not the territory. A clean graphic tells a story, but the story always rests on assumptions that are at best approximately true.

### Exercise 10

Write the last sentence of your summary paragraph. It describes at least one Quantity of Interest and a measure of uncertainty.

```{r temperance-10}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "We estimate that the Neighbors postcard, relative to no postcard, raises the probability of voting by about 8.1 percentage points on average (95% CI: 7.6 to 8.6 pp), with the effect concentrated among Sometimes Vote (8.6 pp) and Always Vote (9.0 pp) voters and notably smaller among Rarely Vote voters (4.4 pp).",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 5)
```

###

Add a final sentence to your summary paragraph, but don't copy/paste exactly. `Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + K`.

### Exercise 11

Write a few sentences explaining why the estimate might be wrong. Suggest alternative estimates if warranted.

```{r temperance-11}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "Three reasons our estimate may be optimistic. First, our 95% confidence intervals are computed at the voter level, but treatment was assigned at the household level --- so the true intervals are mildly wider than what we report. Second, the experiment was conducted in 2006 in Michigan, on voters who had cast a ballot in 2004; the relevance to a 2026 Texas campaign depends on stability holding across both era and geography, and the slope of turnout on Neighbors-style postcards has likely flattened since 2006 as voters have habituated to the technique. Third, we report intent-to-treat (assignment, not delivery) effects --- for the campaign manager weighing the cost-effectiveness of the postcards, this is the right number; for understanding why Neighbors works, it understates the per-recipient effect. A defensible 2026-Texas estimate is a Neighbors effect somewhere between 3 and 8 percentage points, not the 8.1 we measure.",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 10)
```

###

Always go back to your Preceptor Table. Even a perfectly estimated statistical model is rarely as useful as we'd like.

### Exercise 12

Rearrange the material in your QMD so the order is graphic, then summary paragraph. The chunk that creates `fit_shaming` must occur before the chunk that creates the graphic. `Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + K`. At the R prompt, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd")
```

CP/CR.

```{r temperance-12}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "    > show_file(\"analysis.qmd\")\n    ---\n    title: \"Shaming\"\n    author: \"<your name>\"\n    execute:\n      echo: false\n    ---\n    \n    ```{r}\n    #| message: false\n    library(tidyverse)\n    library(tidymodels)\n    library(primer.data)\n    library(broom)\n    library(marginaleffects)\n    ```\n    \n    ```{r}\n    x <- shaming |>\n      mutate(civ_engage = primary_00 + primary_02 + primary_04 +\n                          general_00 + general_02 + general_04) |>\n      select(primary_06, treatment, sex, age, civ_engage) |>\n      drop_na() |>\n      mutate(voter_class = factor(case_when(\n        civ_engage %in% c(5, 6) ~ \"Always Vote\",\n        civ_engage %in% c(3, 4) ~ \"Sometimes Vote\",\n        civ_engage %in% c(1, 2) ~ \"Rarely Vote\"),\n        levels = c(\"Rarely Vote\", \"Sometimes Vote\", \"Always Vote\"))) |>\n      mutate(voted = as.factor(primary_06)) |>\n      drop_na()\n    ```\n    \n    ```{r}\n    #| cache: true\n    fit_shaming <- logistic_reg(engine = \"glm\") |>\n      fit(voted ~ age + sex + treatment*voter_class, data = x)\n    ```\n    \n    ```{r}\n    plot_predictions(extract_fit_engine(fit_shaming),\n                     condition = c(\"treatment\", \"voter_class\"),\n                     draw = FALSE) |>\n      ggplot(aes(x = treatment, y = estimate, color = voter_class)) +\n      geom_pointrange(aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), size = 0.7) +\n      scale_x_discrete(limits = c(\"No Postcard\", \"Civic Duty\", \"Hawthorne\",\n                                  \"Self\", \"Neighbors\")) +\n      scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_percent(), limits = c(0, 0.75)) +\n      facet_wrap(~ voter_class) +\n      labs(title = \"Predicted Turnout by Postcard and Voter Class\",\n           subtitle = \"Neighbors moves Sometimes/Always voters most\",\n           x = NULL, y = NULL, color = \"Voter Class\",\n           caption = \"Source: Gerber, Green, Larimer 2008 via primer.data::shaming\") +\n      theme_minimal() +\n      theme(legend.position = \"none\", axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))\n    ```\n    \n    Voter mobilization is a perennial campaign question. Using the 344,084-voter Gerber-Green-Larimer 2006 randomized field experiment in Michigan, we estimate the average causal effect of the Neighbors postcard on the probability that a registered voter casts a ballot in the August 2006 primary. One concern is that random assignment happened at the household level, so our 95% intervals are mildly optimistic. We model voter turnout as a Bernoulli variable which is a logistic function of treatment, voter class, the treatment-by-voter-class interaction, age, and sex. We estimate that the Neighbors postcard raises the probability of voting by about 8.1 percentage points on average, with the effect concentrated among Sometimes/Always voters (8-9 pp) and notably smaller among Rarely Vote voters (4 pp).",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 14)
```

###

This is the version of your QMD your teacher is most likely to look at closely.

### Exercise 13

Publish your rendered QMD to GitHub Pages. In the Terminal (not the R prompt!), run:

```
quarto publish gh-pages analysis.qmd
```

Copy/paste the resulting URL below.

```{r temperance-13}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "https://<your-github-username>.github.io/shaming/analysis.html",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 2)
```

###

Commit/push everything.

### Exercise 14

Copy/paste the URL to your GitHub repo.

```{r temperance-14}
question_text(NULL,
  message = "https://github.com/<your-github-username>/shaming",
  answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
  allow_retry = FALSE,
  incorrect = NULL,
  rows = 2)
```

###

We can never know all the entries in the Preceptor Table. That knowledge is reserved for God.

The world confronts us. Make decisions we must.


## Summary
###

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
plot_predictions(extract_fit_engine(fit_shaming),
                 condition = c("treatment", "voter_class"),
                 draw = FALSE) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = treatment, y = estimate, color = voter_class)) +
  geom_pointrange(aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high),
                  size = 0.7) +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("No Postcard", "Civic Duty", "Hawthorne",
                              "Self", "Neighbors")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_percent(),
                     limits = c(0, 0.75)) +
  facet_wrap(~ voter_class) +
  labs(
    title    = "Predicted Turnout by Postcard and Voter Class",
    subtitle = "Neighbors moves Sometimes/Always voters most; Rarely Vote barely budges",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = NULL,
    color    = "Voter Class",
    caption  = "Source: Gerber, Green, Larimer 2008 via primer.data::shaming"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))
```

Voter mobilization is a perennial campaign question: which messaging --- if any --- gets registered voters who would otherwise stay home to actually show up. Using the 344,084-voter Gerber-Green-Larimer 2006 randomized field experiment in Michigan, we estimate the average causal effect of the Neighbors postcard on the probability that a registered voter casts a ballot in the August 2006 primary. One concern is that random assignment happened at the household level, not the voter level, so our 95% confidence intervals are mildly optimistic. We model voter turnout as a Bernoulli variable which is a logistic function of treatment, voter class, the treatment-by-voter-class interaction, age, and sex. We estimate that the Neighbors postcard raises the probability of voting by about 8.1 percentage points on average (95% CI: 7.6 to 8.6 pp), with the effect concentrated among Sometimes Vote (8.6 pp) and Always Vote (9.0 pp) voters and notably smaller among Rarely Vote voters (4.4 pp).

This finding would inform a campaign-allocation decision: which voters to target with the Neighbors-style postcard, given a fixed mailing budget. The headline implication is counterintuitive --- the people who needed a turnout nudge most (Rarely Vote) are also the ones a postcard moves least, while the people most likely to vote anyway (Always Vote) are the ones whose marginal turnout is most movable by social pressure. A campaign manager who applied this finding directly would mail Neighbors-style postcards to Sometimes/Always voters and skip the Rarely Vote list. The finding does not address every campaign question --- it says nothing about door-knocking, phone banking, digital ads, or how the effect of social-pressure mail interacts with other GOTV tools running simultaneously. But it gives a defensible starting point.

The world is always more uncertain than our models would have us believe.

```{r download-answers, child = system.file("child_documents/download_answers.Rmd", package = "tutorial.helpers")}
```
